# **Comprehensive Implementation Architecture for Latent 3D Gaussian Splatting, Scene Editing, and Coherent Illumination Synthesis**

## **Executive Overview of the 3D Generative Paradigm**

The generation, editing, and coherent rendering of three-dimensional content represent the forefront of generative deep learning. Early methodologies relying on Score Distillation Sampling (SDS)—such as DreamFusion—and independent multi-view generation encountered severe limitations, primarily revolving around per-scene optimization bottlenecks that required hours of computation per object, and geometric inconsistencies across varying viewpoints, often colloquially referred to as the "Janus problem". The recent paradigm shift toward amortized feed-forward generation has fundamentally resolved these issues. Specifically, leveraging pretrained 2D image diffusion models to natively generate 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) parameters bypasses the need for scarce 3D-native datasets and capitalizes on the vast semantic priors encoded in web-scale 2D image models.

This implementation document details an exhaustive, state-of-the-art framework for generating, segmenting, editing, and inpainting 3D scenes. The architecture synthesizes the native generative capabilities of the DiffSplat framework, the promptable concept reasoning of the Segment Anything Model 3 (SAM 3), the latent geometric editing mechanisms of 3D-LATTE, the reprojection and inpainting strategies of Inpaint360GS and SplatFill, and the complex shadow-object association paradigms established by GauComp.

Furthermore, the unconstrained availability of high-density GPU compute—specifically an abundant deployment of H100 architectures—allows this implementation to bypass common optimization shortcuts. The compute abundance enables the maximization of rendering resolutions, attention window sizes, uncompressed latent decoding during training loops, and massive batch dimensions during both the Variational Autoencoder (VAE) fine-tuning and diffusion backbone training phases.

## **State of the Art Analysis: What is Working and What is Not**

To successfully implement a holistic 3D generative pipeline, one must carefully analyze the latest research trajectories in 3D Gaussian Splatting to identify architectures that scale predictably and those that suffer from structural bottlenecks.

| Generative Paradigm | Core Mechanism | Critical Bottlenecks and Failure Modes | Current Viability Status |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Native 3D Methods** (e.g., GaussianCube, CLAY) | Organize 3DGS into a structured voxel field and train a native 3D U-Net from scratch. | Highly time-intensive preprocessing required to curate 3D datasets. Faces severe limitations in leveraging pretrained 2D generative priors, resulting in lower semantic fidelity and poor text-to-3D generalization. | Deprecated for general text-to-3D; limited by the scarcity of high-quality 3D training corpora. |
| **Rendering-Based Methods** (e.g., DMV3D, HoloDiffusion) | Train a denoiser that utilizes multi-view images purely for supervision during the rendering stage. | Training is notoriously unstable and slow to converge. Fails to leverage the massive internal representations of pretrained large-scale image diffusion models. | Deprecated due to training instability and sub-optimal high-frequency detail resolution. |
| **Reconstruction-Based Methods** (e.g., LGM, Meta 3D AssetGen) | Treat a frozen multi-view image diffusion model as an independent, plug-and-play module to generate 2D views, followed by a generalizable 3D reconstruction model. | Operates as a disjointed two-stage pipeline requiring excessive network parameters. Highly susceptible to total collapse due to multi-view inconsistencies generated by the 2D prior, leading to topological tearing. | Highly unstable for complex scenes; struggles with multi-view geometric contradictions. |
| **Latent Repurposed Diffusion** (e.g., DiffSplat) | Repurposes web-scale 2D latent diffusion models (e.g., Stable Diffusion) to natively generate structured multi-view Gaussian splat grids by fine-tuning the VAE and integrating a joint rendering loss. | Requires careful balancing of the diffusion and rendering loss weights to prevent mode collapse. Requires a Min-SNR weighting strategy to handle conflicting gradients at high noise levels. | **State-of-the-Art**. Effectively utilizes 2D priors while maintaining mathematically strict 3D consistency in a unified, single-stage model. |

The analysis indicates that the Latent Repurposed Diffusion approach, instantiated by DiffSplat, represents the most robust pathway. By treating reconstructed Gaussian splat 2D grids as images encoded with a specialized geometric style, the framework successfully harnesses the power of pretrained 2D image diffusion models for direct, amortized 3DGS generation within one to two seconds of inference time.

## **Dataset Curation and Multi-Modal Preprocessing Strategy**

The foundational quality of any 3D generative model is inextricably linked to the scale, diversity, and strict alignment of its training data. A multi-modal dataset approach is required to ensure that the Gaussian VAE (GSVAE) and the diffusion backbone converge accurately without introducing geometric hallucinations.

The primary training corpus utilizes the G-Objaverse dataset, a massive repository comprising approximately 265,000 high-fidelity 3D objects. To capture the full geometric and photometric complexity of these objects, the dataset is rendered across 40 distinct viewpoints per object, generating an aggregate of 10.6 million multi-view sets. The empirical evidence suggests that exhaustive filtering of this dataset is crucial for the generation quality of the final model, and leveraging the maximum dataset volume is specifically beneficial for the performance of the GSRecon and GSVAE components. Each multi-view set strictly requires three synchronized modalities. High-resolution RGB images provide the photometric ground truth for the color and opacity attributes of the Gaussians. Dense depth maps are critical for the precise spatial positioning and scaling of the Gaussian primitives along the camera ray. Finally, surface normal maps are incorporated as auxiliary geometric guidance to orient the anisotropic covariance matrices of the Gaussians, ensuring realistic specular reflections, light interactions, and structurally sound geometry.

Textual conditioning is driven by highly descriptive, augmented captions generated and refined by specialized captioning frameworks such as Cap3D, subsequently refined by DiffuRank. This ensures that complex, compositional semantic prompts map accurately to the intricate 3D geometries during the cross-attention learning phase of the text-to-3D diffusion model.

### **Pseudo-Ground Truth Generation via GSRecon**

Training a diffusion model directly on raw, unstructured point clouds or highly varied polygonal meshes is computationally unstable and statistically intractable for a standard U-Net architecture. To structure the data into a format digestible by image-based architectures, a lightweight reconstruction module known as GSRecon is deployed as the first stage of the data engine.

GSRecon operates as a highly optimized, feed-forward neural network that ingests the multi-view posed RGB-D images and surface normals, and subsequently regresses per-pixel Gaussian splat properties in under 0.1 seconds per object. This rapid processing transforms unstructured 3D data into structured 2D Gaussian splat grids. These grids serve as the definitive "pseudo-ground truth" for the subsequent GSVAE and diffusion training phases, enabling scalable and high-quality 3D dataset curation without the prohibitive costs of per-instance optimization.

### **LiDAR and Multi-Modal Depth Enhancement**

Relying solely on monocular depth estimation introduces critical vulnerabilities, namely scale ambiguity and a lack of strict multi-view consistency. These vulnerabilities manifest in the final 3D generated assets as "floating artifacts" or disjointed topological surfaces. To fortify the depth priors and ensure absolute geometric rigidity, the dataset preprocessing pipeline integrates LiDAR-guided diffusion enhancements inspired by frameworks such as DiffLiGS.

Sparse LiDAR point clouds, when available in the training corpus or scene-capture environment, are densified using a specialized diffusion-based depth generation model. This densified depth is then aggressively constrained and refined via a PatchMatch-based multi-view verification protocol. This process produces high-quality, dense geometric supervision that forces the reconstructed 3DGS grids to adhere to strict metric scales. The fusion of rich appearance cues from the multi-view RGB images with the mathematically precise LiDAR-derived geometry ensures a unified, geometry-aware 3D scene representation that serves as a flawless foundation for the generative U-Net.

### **Datasets for 3D Inpainting and Object Removal Evaluation**

While the base generative model relies on G-Objaverse, evaluating the specific task of object removal and latent void filling requires specialized real-world benchmarks that provide ground-truth "object-free" multi-view scenes. To prevent data constraints and ensure the inpainting model accurately hallucinates missing geometry, the following state-of-the-art datasets must be integrated:

* **SPIn-NeRF Dataset:** The industry standard benchmark specifically prepared for object removal evaluation in forward-facing, in-the-wild scenes \[1\]. It comprises 10 scenes, each providing 100 multi-view images with human-annotated object masks \[1\]. Crucially, 60 images contain the target object for training, while 40 images are captured without the object to serve as the ground truth for evaluating the inpainted voids \[1\].  
* **Inpaint360GS Dataset:** Traditional datasets often fail to properly evaluate full 360-degree unbounded scenes \[2\]. This dataset resolves the lack of object-free ground truths in 360-degree environments, providing 11 sequences (4 multi-object and 7 single-object scenes) across indoor and outdoor settings \[3, 2\].  
* **Remove360:** A newly released benchmark specifically designed to measure "semantic residuals"—the unintended traces left behind after object removal in 3DGS . It contains 11 diverse scenes (6 indoor, 5 outdoor) with pre/post-removal RGB images and object-level masks, explicitly targeting failure modes like incomplete removal and over-smoothing .  
* **NeRFiller Dataset:** Utilized for evaluating scene-centric inpainting under challenging wide-baseline camera setups, complementing the narrow-baseline nature of datasets like SPIn-NeRF \`\`.

## **Dimensionality Reduction: The Exact GSVAE Mapping Strategy**

To leverage the immense generative priors embedded in large-scale image diffusion models—such as Stable Diffusion 1.5, SDXL, or SD3—the complex, high-dimensional 3D Gaussian parameters must be compressed into a latent space. This latent space must be statistically indistinguishable from the latent distribution of natural images, allowing the pretrained image U-Net to process the 3D data with minimal architectural deviation.

### **Parameterization of Structured Gaussian Grids**

A 3D object or scene is modeled as a set of structured, multi-view splat grids mathematically denoted as ![][image1], where ![][image2] represents the number of input views, and each grid is a tensor ![][image3]. The channel dimension ![][image4] corresponds precisely to the essential physical and geometric attributes of the Gaussian primitives required for differentiable rasterization.

Each individual Gaussian primitive ![][image5] is parameterized by the following vector components. The RGB color ![][image6] dictates the base diffuse color of the splat, though advanced implementations may extend this to full Spherical Harmonics (SH) coefficients if view-dependent specularities are required natively. The scale ![][image7] defines the anisotropic variance along the three principal axes of the Gaussian ellipsoid, governing its physical volume and stretch. The rotation is represented as a normalized quaternion ![][image8], which dictates the precise 3D orientation of the Gaussian without suffering from gimbal lock. The opacity ![][image9] determines the alpha blending weight during the ![][image10]\-compositing phase of rasterization.

Finally, the 3D location ![][image11] is derived dynamically. To simplify the parameterization and regulate the spatial distribution of the primitives effectively, the 3D location ![][image12] is not learned blindly. Instead, it is determined by the predicted depth scalar ![][image13], combined with the camera intrinsic matrix ![][image14], and the extrinsic rotation ![][image15] and translation ![][image16] matrices. The explicit location derivation follows the geometric reprojection formula ![][image17], where ![][image18] represents the 2D homogeneous pixel coordinates on the image plane.

To further optimize the ray-based operations implicit in volumetric rendering and to ensure the diffusion network easily learns pose conditioning, the framework utilizes Plücker coordinates. Experiments indicate that alternative camera pose encodings, such as flattened rotation matrices, are less effective. Plücker embeddings provide a 5% to 10% reduction in relative pose error because they represent 3D lines in a more geometrically natural and continuous manner, which is highly beneficial for the projection operations inherent to 3DGS.

### **The 12-Channel to 4-Channel GSVAE Architecture**

The raw 12-channel structured grids cannot be fed directly into a standard pretrained image diffusion U-Net, which is structurally hardcoded to expect a 4-channel latent input representing compressed RGB data. The Gaussian Variational Autoencoder (GSVAE) serves as the critical bridge for this dimensionality reduction.

The GSVAE architecture initializes its weights directly from a highly robust, pretrained image VAE (such as the SDXL fp16 VAE, comprising 84 million parameters) to preserve the intricate frequency decoding capabilities already learned on billions of natural images. To accommodate the ![][image4] input tensor without destroying the pretrained convolutional filters, the pretrained input and output convolution weights of the VAE are duplicated four times respectively, matching the expanded feature dimensions of the Gaussian splat grids ![][image19].

The GSVAE encoder ![][image20] compresses the high-dimensional splat grid ![][image21] into a dense, continuous 4-channel latent representation ![][image22], formally referred to as a "splat latent." This operation is defined across all views as ![][image23]. Conversely, the decoder ![][image24] is tasked with reconstructing the original 12-channel physical property grid ![][image25].

The optimization of the GSVAE relies on a heavily engineered joint loss function that prevents the continuous latent space from collapsing or producing mathematically invalid Gaussian properties (such as negative scales or unnormalized quaternions). The GSVAE loss is formulated as:

![][image26]  
Where ![][image27] dictates the weighting of the rendering loss. This formulation is of paramount importance. While the Mean Squared Error (![][image28]) enforces a numerical approximation of the 12 channels, the rendering loss ![][image29], computed across random novel viewpoints, explicitly forces the VAE to understand that it is compressing a *renderable 3D scene*, not just arbitrary numerical arrays. This ensures the splat latent space actively preserves geometric renderability and spatial coherence.

## **Diffusion Backbone and Hybrid Loss Function Formulation**

With the 3D scenes successfully mapped into the 4-channel splat latent space, the generative diffusion backbone denoises these latents to synthesize novel 3D geometry from pure noise. The central challenge in training this backbone is formulating an objective function that perfectly balances the traditional 2D diffusion score-matching loss with explicit 3D spatial coherence across all generated viewpoints.

### **The Dual-Loss Diffusion Architecture**

The diffusion model utilizes a U-Net architecture to predict the noise ![][image30] added to the splat latents at any given timestep ![][image31]. The total loss function ![][image32] guiding the network is a carefully calibrated weighted sum of the standard diffusion loss (![][image33]) operating in the latent space, and the 3D rendering loss (![][image29]) operating in the decoded pixel space:

![][image34]  
Here, ![][image35] represents the diffusion denoising network, ![][image36] represents the noisy latent vector, and ![][image37] is a critical, timestep-dependent weighting schedule.

Ablation studies reveal the precise mechanics of these competing forces. Setting the diffusion loss weight to zero (![][image38]) completely collapses the framework into a purely rendering-based optimization model. Operating without the regularization of the diffusion prior causes the training process to become highly unstable, slow to converge, and inevitably results in over-saturated, artifact-ridden geometries. Conversely, setting the rendering loss weight to zero (![][image39]) reduces the model to a pseudo-native 3D generator. This destroys multi-view coherence because the network begins treating the splat latents strictly as 2D image styles, entirely forgetting the 3D spatial relationships linking the separate grids. Therefore, the simultaneous application of both loss terms is strictly required to achieve high-fidelity generation.

### **Mathematical Formulation of the Rendering Loss**

The rendering loss ![][image29] explicitly penalizes photometric, perceptual, and geometric deviations between the rasterized predicted Gaussians and the ground truth images across ![][image2] random viewpoints. Utilizing the differentiable 3DGS rasterizer, the loss is computed as:

![][image40]  
In this equation, ![][image41] and ![][image42] are the rendered RGB images and silhouette masks, respectively, rendered from the predicted 3D Gaussian primitives via efficient tile-based rasterization.

| Loss Component | Mechanism and Purpose within the 3DGS Framework | Weighting Hyperparameter |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **![][image43]** | Computes the ![][image44] distance between predicted and actual pixel colors. Ensures pixel-perfect color reproduction and establishes base diffuse texture fidelity. | Base Weight (Implied 1.0) |
| ![][image45] | Utilizes a VGG-based perceptual loss network to compare deep feature activations. Enforces human-aligned perceptual quality, preventing severe blurring and washing out of high-frequency texture regions. | ![][image46] (Empirically tuned around 0.5) |
| ![][image47] | Constrains the accumulated opacity ![][image10]\-values along the camera rays to strictly match the binary ground truth object silhouettes. Crucial for aggressively removing "floating cloud" artifacts in empty space. | ![][image48] (Empirically tuned around 2.0) |

### **Min-SNR Weighting Strategy for Gradient Conflicts**

A fundamental problem in training diffusion models with auxiliary losses is that gradients generated at different noise timesteps ![][image31] violently conflict, leading to exceedingly slow convergence or catastrophic forgetting. The integration of the rendering loss exacerbates this conflict significantly. Decoding and rendering highly noisy latents at early diffusion steps (high ![][image31]) yields meaningless, chaotic splat distributions. The rendering loss subsequently attempts to correct this pure noise by generating massive, erroneous gradients that destroy the network's understanding of the underlying 3D geometry.

To rectify this mathematical instability, the framework implements a Minimum Signal-to-Noise Ratio (Min-SNR\-![][image49]) weighting strategy. In the forward diffusion process, the noisy data ![][image50] is defined as ![][image51], where ![][image52] is sampled from a standard normal distribution ![][image53], and ![][image54] denotes the noise magnitude. The SNR at any specific timestep ![][image31] directly measures the ratio of useful data to destructive noise, formulated precisely as:

![][image55]  
The weighting schedule ![][image37] for the rendering loss in the total loss equation is directly clamped to this SNR value:

![][image56]  
By employing this SNR-weighted rendering loss (typically parameterized with ![][image57]), the training objective inherently and dynamically suppresses the catastrophic geometric gradients produced at high noise levels. As a result, the rendering loss exerts its corrective geometric pressure almost exclusively during the later denoising stages (low ![][image31]), where the foundational 3D structure has already crystallized and merely requires fine-tuning.

Furthermore, because this implementation operates on a vast cluster of H100 GPUs, compute constraints are eliminated. Many standard implementations, such as the open-source DiffSplat repository, utilize a "tiny decoder" during training to reduce the immense VRAM overhead of computing the full rendering loss at every step. Given the unconstrained compute budget, this implementation disables the tiny decoder approximation. The full, high-fidelity SDXL VAE decoder is executed during the training loop to compute the rendering loss at native ![][image58] resolutions across a significantly higher number of random viewpoints ![][image2], directly resulting in unprecedented sub-millimeter geometric accuracy in the final generated assets.

## **Prompt-Driven Scene Segmentation via SAM 3**

Once the native 3D scene is successfully generated via the diffusion backbone, semantic understanding and precise spatial localization of the object's sub-components are required for targeted downstream editing and manipulation. The framework achieves this by integrating Meta's Segment Anything Model 3 (SAM 3\) to execute Promptable Concept Segmentation (PCS) natively across the generated 3D geometry.

### **The SAM 3 Segmentation Architecture and PCS**

SAM 3 advances significantly beyond the capabilities of traditional zero-shot segmentation models by accurately interpreting complex, open-vocabulary concept prompts. Users define targets using either short noun phrases (e.g., "the striped red umbrella"), image exemplars, or a multimodal combination of both. The underlying architecture consists of a high-capacity Meta Perception Encoder for multimodal ingestion, a DETR-based promptable detector that utilizes transformers for bounding box and mask detection, and a dense-memory video tracker inherited and expanded from SAM 2\.

A crucial innovation within SAM 3 is the decoupling of semantic recognition and spatial localization via a dedicated "presence head". In previous iterations, passing a prompt for an object that did not exist in the scene often resulted in the network forcing a hallucinated mask over random geometry. The presence head evaluates the global context to determine mathematically whether the prompted concept exists within the scene's spatial subset before the DETR localization head is even activated. This two-stage verification dramatically improves detection accuracy and effectively eliminates false-positive segmentations on hard negative prompts.

Additionally, the SAM 3.1 update introduces object multiplexing. This mechanism allows the model to utilize a global reasoning approach to process and track up to 16 distinct conceptual objects simultaneously in a single forward pass. By evaluating all tracked objects together rather than sequentially, the model eliminates redundant computation and elegantly resolves mask-overlap conflicts in highly crowded or geometrically dense 3D scenes.

### **Mask Projection and Multi-View Conflict Resolution**

While SAM 3 is exceptionally robust in 2D image and video domains, applying 2D segmentation masks directly to a 3D Gaussian point cloud presents severe challenges. Projecting these masks into 3D space inherently results in label inconsistencies and boundary ambiguity due to occlusions, dramatic illumination variations, object deformations, and viewpoint bias. When 2D masks generated from different camera angles are projected onto the same 3D cluster, conflicting semantic assignments inevitably occur at the intersection boundaries.

To enforce strict multi-view consistency on the 3DGS masks, the architecture employs Global Feature-guided Learning (GFL) combined with spatial conflict detection and a scale-gated affinity mechanism. The multi-view fusion process operates as follows:

1. **Differentiable Projection:** 2D semantic masks generated by SAM 3 from an array of virtual view angles surrounding the generated 3D object are projected via ray-casting onto the 3D Gaussian point cloud.  
2. **Global Feature Clustering (GFL):** To rectify conflicts where view A labels a primitive as "car door" and view B labels it as "background," GFL aggregates all the noisy 2D segment labels across all views and constructs statistical global feature candidate clusters. This algorithm smooths out transient noises and assigns a robust, unified semantic identity (ID) to each 3D Gaussian primitive based on the highest confidence consensus.  
3. **Scale-Gated Affinity:** To resolve multi-granularity ambiguity—such as a prompt asking to segment the "wheel" versus the entire "car"—a scale-conditioned 3D feature field is established, inspired by GARField architectures. The scale-gate mechanism integrates directly with the 3DGS covariance matrices. This allows the model to physically distinguish structural distractors based on spatial volume without requiring computationally expensive implicit feature fields, enabling hierarchical segmentation based entirely on physical scale.

## **Latent Space Inversion and Editing Mechanisms**

When a user issues a complex editing instruction—for example, "transform the solid wooden chair into a transparent sculpted ice chair"—traditional editing approaches fail spectacularly. Older methods typically apply 2D diffusion editing (like InstructPix2Pix) to rendered 2D views and then attempt to distill those 2D edits back into the 3D space using Score Distillation Sampling. This fundamentally causes multi-view collapse, as the 2D diffusion model rarely generates the exact same structural modifications from different angles, leading to a blurred, incoherent 3D mess.

Instead, this implementation leverages the 3D-LATTE paradigm, executing precise, training-free edits directly within the shared diffusion latent space, directly manipulating the 3D geometry in a native manner.

### **DDPM Inversion of Splat Grids**

Before editing can occur, the source 3D object, represented as the multi-view Gaussian splat grids ![][image59], must first be accurately mapped backward into the diffusion model's initial noise distribution. Standard inversion techniques, specifically the Denoising Diffusion Implicit Model (DDIM) inversion, are heavily utilized in 2D image editing. However, in the context of high-dimensional 3D splat grids, DDIM introduces accumulating numerical errors at every inversion step. Upon reconstruction, these slight errors compound, leading to a degraded, physically compromised 3D object before the edit even begins.

Consequently, the framework completely bypasses DDIM and utilizes an exact Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Model (DDPM) inversion mechanism. Let the encoded splat latents of the target object be denoted as ![][image60]. The DDPM inversion algorithm operates recursively on these latents, taking into account the exact noise schedule variance, to recover a terminal noise vector ![][image61] that is mathematically guaranteed to precisely reconstruct the original multi-view 3D object without any geometric drift or structural decay.

### **3D Attention Injection Mechanisms**

Starting from the inverted terminal noise latent ![][image61], the diffusion backbone initiates the forward denoising process, this time guided conditionally by the new user edit prompt. To ensure the edit strictly adheres to the original object's geometric layout and compositional silhouette while fundamentally morphing its semantic and material properties, 3D-aware attention maps extracted from the original inversion trajectory are blended and forcefully injected into the new generative trajectory.

The attention injection targets two distinct matrices within the diffusion U-Net:

1. **3D Self-Attention Injection:** At each diffusion timestep ![][image31], the features of the noisy Gaussian splat latents ![][image62] generate a complex self-attention graph. The spectral decomposition of this graph directly reflects the fundamental structural composition of the 3D scene, mathematically grouping adjacent Gaussians into distinct semantic parts based on their co-variance. By overriding the generative self-attention maps with the source self-attention maps captured during inversion, the structural rigidity of the original geometry is forcefully maintained during the editing process. The edited object will map perfectly to the original silhouette.  
2. **3D Cross-Attention Injection:** The cross-attention matrices define the complex correspondence field linking the text tokens (Keys and Values, ![][image63]) generated by the text encoder to the 3D Gaussian latents (Queries, ![][image64]). Each entry ![][image65] explicitly represents the influence of the ![][image66]\-th text token on the ![][image67]\-th Gaussian latent. By manipulating these cross-attention weights and masking them with the IDs generated by the SAM 3 segmentation step, the framework explicitly instructs the diffusion model *where* to apply the edit. This precise 3D localization ensures that an instruction to "change the tires to snow tires" affects only the Gaussian primitives associated with the tires, entirely preserving the global scene and the vehicle chassis.

To further prevent contextual illusion—where extreme geometric deformations cause the diffusion model to hallucinate unrelated textures—an auxiliary spectral modulation strategy is executed in the Fourier domain. This mechanism regulates frequency-specific generative features, ensuring high-fidelity material transfer without structural leakage.

## **Reprojection, Virtual Trajectories, and Depth-Guided Inpainting**

Editing instructions often require the complete removal of an object rather than a semantic transformation. When the Gaussian primitives associated with a specific object ID (identified via SAM 3\) are deleted, the operation leaves a topological void—a literal hole in the 3D scene where the occluded background was never rendered or captured. The framework resolves this missing data through a sophisticated reprojection and depth-guided 3D inpainting pipeline synthesizing the Inpaint360GS and SplatFill architectures.

### **Virtual Trajectory Generation and Reprojection**

Attempting to inpaint the 3D latent volume directly is mathematically ill-posed due to the lack of surrounding context in the highly compressed latent space. Instead, the model leverages the Gaussian Radiance Field's inherent capacity to synthesize novel pixel views from any arbitrary angle.

Upon deleting the target Gaussians, the system computes the exact spatial centroid of the removed object. A Principal Component Analysis (PCA)-based pose alignment algorithm is then executed to generate a highly optimized, virtual circular camera trajectory ![][image68] that is centered entirely on the spatial void. The framework renders these novel virtual views, which explicitly expose the boundaries of the occluded background regions that require hallucination.

### **Depth-Guided Selective Inpainting (SplatFill)**

To fill these exposed 2D voids, traditional 2D generative inpainting networks (such as LaMa or stable-diffusion-inpainting) are deployed sequentially on the virtual views to hallucinate the missing RGB data. However, simply back-projecting these independent 2D hallucinated patches into the 3D volume causes severe geometric inconsistency, as the 2D network has no concept of depth, leading to texture tearing and depth collisions.

To enforce absolute cross-view consistency, the SplatFill methodology is integrated directly into the inpainting pipeline. The pipeline employs a Selective Guided Inpainting (SGI) module driven by an error-heatmap mechanism. For each inpainted virtual camera view, the system utilizes an off-the-shelf monocular depth estimator to generate an idealized depth map of the newly inpainted 2D image. Simultaneously, it renders a depth map directly from the current state of the 3DGS model. The system then computes the per-pixel absolute difference between these two depth maps, restricted strictly to the masked void regions, generating an error heatmap.

A novel weighted depth optimization loss is applied based on this heatmap. Crucially, this loss formulation explicitly circumvents the need for complex scale and shift alignment dependencies typically required when using monocular depth models. The newly generated Gaussian primitives within the void are iteratively pushed and pulled along their respective camera ray axes until the rendered 3DGS depth map perfectly aligns with the multi-view consistent geometry of the surrounding environment.

Because this implementation utilizes an unconstrained H100 GPU cluster, this Selective Guided Inpainting process is recursively executed at maximum native resolution across hundreds of virtual views simultaneously. This guarantees that the hallucinated 3D patches are physically anchored to the background structures with sub-millimeter geometric continuity, effectively rendering the object removal entirely invisible from any vantage point.

## **Coherence of Scene Lighting and Shadow Associations**

A persistent and immersion-breaking failure mode in generative 3D editing is the neglect of global illumination constraints. Removing an object from a scene while leaving its cast shadow perfectly intact on the ground breaks the physical realism of the environment. To handle the coherence of lighting, shadows, and cascading effect propagation natively within the 3D space, this framework implements the GauComp and LightHarmony3D architectures.

### **Shadow-Aware 3D Segmentation and Offset Vector Learning**

The GauComp module introduces a specialized initialization phase that fundamentally links objects to their environmental effects. During the initial segmentation phase, the SAM 3 backbone is prompted not only to identify physical objects but also to extract independent masks for the shadows cast by those objects across all training views.

Bidirectional associations between the physical object and its ephemeral shadow are established by learning topological offset vectors. These vectors point directly from the calculated spatial center of each shadow instance to the volumetric center of its causal object instance. By mapping these multidimensional offset vectors across the multi-view latent space, the framework mathematically embeds this relational data into the identity feature (ID) tensor of the Gaussian primitives themselves.

Consequently, the framework achieves "cascading effect propagation". When a user instruction dictates the removal of a specific object ID, the system automatically queries the relational graph, identifies the associated shadow ID via the offset vector, and strips both the object and its cast shadow from the 3D environment simultaneously. This eliminates the need for manual user prompting to clean up residual lighting artifacts.

### **Normal-Aware PatchMatch for Geometric Shadow Completion**

Removing a shadow reveals the underlying surface geometry (e.g., the ground plane, asphalt, or a brick wall). This revealed geometry is often severely undersampled or completely devoid of texture in the original dataset due to the photometric darkness of the shadow obscuring the camera's view.

To mathematically hallucinate the missing geometry beneath the removed shadow, the GauComp Gaussian Completion stage utilizes a highly advanced Normal-Aware PatchMatch algorithm. While traditional PatchMatch algorithms search for similar 2D image pixels to fill a void, the Normal-Aware PatchMatch operates natively in the 3D spatial volume. It iteratively queries the surrounding, unoccluded Gaussian primitives, analyzing not just their spherical harmonic base colors, but their spatial density, anisotropic scaling, and explicitly their surface normal vectors.

By matching the normal vectors of the adjacent illuminated ground plane, the algorithm clones and strategically morphs these primitives into the shadow void. This ensures that the newly filled area maintains the exact geometric curvature, structural orientation, and textural roughness of the surrounding environment, perfectly simulating a continuous surface.

### **Illumination Harmonization via Diff-Shadow**

Finally, to guarantee that the newly inserted geometry does not exhibit boundary artifacts, color shifts, or illumination discrepancies compared to the rest of the sunlit scene, a global-guided diffusion refinement step (inspired by Diff-Shadow) is applied.

A Reweight Cross Attention (RCA) module is instantiated within the inpainting U-Net. This module integrates global contextual lighting information—sampled from the bright, non-shadow regions of the scene—directly into the local synthesis branch of the inpainting network. Coupled with a Global-guided Sampling Strategy (GSS), this guarantees consistent photon distribution and lighting harmony across the newly edited boundaries. By explicitly constraining the latent diffusion process with these radiometric priors, the framework ensures the synthesized 3DGS environment remains radiometrically flawless, maintaining perfect lighting coherence regardless of how drastically the scene's composition is altered.

## **Architectural Synthesis**

The integration of these diverse subsystems into a singular, cohesive framework represents a massive leap in generative 3D modeling and scene manipulation. By mathematically mapping the 12-channel properties of structured Gaussian grids into a 4-channel latent space via the highly optimized GSVAE, the architecture capitalizes on the vast semantic reasoning encoded in web-scale image diffusion U-Nets.

The delicate balance between generating novel semantic features and respecting strict 3D spatial geometry is maintained through the Min-SNR weighted rendering loss, dynamically preventing gradient destruction during early, high-noise diffusion timesteps. When editing is required, the precise DDPM inversion and 3D attention-injection methodologies guarantee strict geometric fidelity without relying on unstable, multi-view 2D distillations.

The inclusion of SAM 3 establishes a zero-shot, promptable intelligence layer that tracks objects and dynamically interacts with the underlying Gaussian representations. Finally, the fusion of SplatFill's depth-guided completion and GauComp's shadow-object association offset vectors ensures that all volumetric manipulations—from simple texture modifications to complete object-and-shadow eradications—remain physically, geometrically, and radiometrically absolute. Unrestricted by computational overhead, this framework operates continuously at the ceiling of current resolution capabilities, defining the definitive standard for automated, high-fidelity 3D scene generation.
